data.title: Kimberly-Clark: The Kenvue Deal Could Be Used To Cut The Dividend - Strong Sell
data.description:
Intro

Founded in 1872 originally as a paper mill partnership, Kimberly-Clark (KMB) has evolved into a personal care and consumer hygiene powerhouse. It has been in business for more than 150 years and today supplies over 175 countries worldwide. KMB holds the number one or two position in 70 markets. Its business model spins around defensive categories like baby and child care, feminine care, adult care, and family care. Historically, the most transformative deal was the 1995 merger with Scott Paper Company, which laid the groundwork for today’s core business.

Iconic brands today include, among others, Huggies diapers, Kleenex tissues (what I associate KMB primarily with), Depend adult incontinence solutions, and Kimberly-Clark Professional restroom paper towels.

The defensive business model offers not a spectacular but a steady and reliable demand story. The expectation should be, under normal circumstances, predictable cash flow that leads to a compounding, dividend-paying stock.

While the latter is the case, unfortunately, KMB stock is down 16% over the last ten years on a nominal basis, not counting inflation. Dividends of course come on top, but shares of such a company being down so strongly are rather a sign that something might be wrong.

And indeed, KMB has had issues with growth. The final trigger leading to the sharp share price decline recently was the announced acquisition of Kenvue (KVUE), a company I had written about in the past, too.

As I am not convinced of the deal and as I primarily see more potential for the destruction of shareholder value than credible upside, especially via a potential dividend cut, I rate KMB stock a sell.

Huggies and Tylenol — a Combo to create Headaches

To prove the point that KMB, in my view, is a no-growth company, let’s look at a few charts to quickly get that out of the way. First, total sales over the last ten years notched up minimally when lockdowns created higher speculative demand and consequently shortages (excessive stockpiling), while the subsequent inflationary wave brought one-time price adjustments.

However, since then, the top-line development has been meager, to put it mildly. Over the last five years (rolling twelve month basis), sales practically went flat. Looking closer, we can see that the last few quarters saw even declining sales — not good.

Margins tell us the rest we need to know. First, the sudden spike in inflation temporarily depressed gross margins as input costs could not be pushed quick enough into the market. This happened only over time. By mid-2024, gross margins were back to the previous high.

However, since then, they began to drop again, indicating cost and pricing pressure instead of steady demand and stable operations.

Operating margins confirm this view, although the drop was a bit later but significantly sharper.

The latest earnings release tells us that sales were affected by some minor divestments, which I am okay with. Nothing to complain about.

However, gross margins indeed dropped due to a highly competitive business environment — the cost-of-living crisis hits everywhere. In many of my other analyses, I had written about the erosion of brand values and, as a result, limited pricing power for companies that were thought to be able to hike prices as they liked. This is clearly not the case. Premium strategies are fine, but unfortunately their significance and strength have been a bit overestimated.

Many consumers have an eye on prices and the value they get. If a company overshoots with pricing to protect margins, the consequence is lower volumes, and in the worst case, lower sales (the latter not applicable here).

Two more points, before we discuss the Kenvue deal. Debt and free cash flow. Net debt has even fallen quite a bit from over $8 billion to $6.7 billion per the last count. Maybe this made management confident to look for growth elsewhere via an acquisition — more further below.

Operating cash flow (orange bars) and free cash flow (green line) below unfortunately have chosen to follow the direction of margins, namely to the downside. At least, this is what it looks like at first sight.

We can see that these unusual swings in cash flows above were the result of a few bigger working capital shifts, first up (working capital release), then down (WC buildup).

To not make it too complicated and to smooth the swings out, let’s wrap it up this way: growth is practically not there, margins are currently under pressure, and operating cash flow on a working capital adjusted basis is somewhere around $2.5 billion USD. Free cash flow after Capex of $700–$900 million realistically is somewhere around $1.6–1.8 billion USD.

Unfortunately, this level is dangerously close to the dividend payout which coincidentally is likewise $1.6 billion.

Initially, I had not connected the dots this way, but I think the Kenvue deal appears in an entirely different light given the above — KMB, itself a dividend king with 53 years of consecutive annual raises (and KVUE in that sense likewise, having been split from dividend king J&J), obviously does not want to cut its dividend. That’s why I think they are acquiring cash flow and an opportunity to “improve” the dividend coverage.

The Kenvue deal

I divided this chapter into two subtopics we need to discuss, in my opinion.

The Deal Structure (assuming it closes)

Note: Yes, first and foremost, I assume the deal will close; else everything below will become irrelevant.

If regulators give their green light for an expected H2 2026 close, Kimberly-Clark's $48.7 billion bid ($3.50 in cash for KVUE stockholders, rest in equity) will create a new combined entity being owned 54% by current KMB and 46% by current KVUE equity holders. In other words, old KMB shareholders will see their ownership almost cut in half to buy a company I’ve seen being in trouble with a high probability for a dividend cut in the not-too-distant future.

While current KVUE shareholders might see some upside from the current $17 share price to the deal price of ~$21, I am not so sure about the combined company post-deal close.

The Rationale, Expected Synergies, and … a dividend cut?

At its core, Kimberly-Clark intends to create a holistic consumer health and wellness leader: from toddlers to seniors, KMB plans to offer the full spectrum in its niche for every age group.

Pairing its absorbent, daily-ritual staples (think Huggies for babies, Depend for seniors) with Kenvue's health elixirs (Tylenol, Neutrogena, Listerine) to tie consumers from cradle to golden years.

As the deal presentation shows, the combination would create the world’s second-biggest such entity, trailing only P&G (PG) by sales.

The rationale for the deal is to expand globally and put together both companies's geographic strengths. There are a few overlaps offering potential synergies, plus many markets where only one of them is currently present. This shall support the expansion into new regions by making use of existing distribution channels of the respective other.

As with every deal of bigger size, synergies are a key topic to sell the deal to shareholders. Efficiency improvements are aimed to save around $2 billion in costs on a run-rate basis. 80% of this goal is promised to be achieved in two years post-deal close. EPS are expected to be accretive by year two.

However, I am not buying the synergy projections, respectively not putting them into my calculations. First, the deal is still far out into the future (assuming it happens). Until then, costs could rise further and / or the competitive landscape could intensify, making the current projections potentially too optimistic — inflation is sticky, and consumer confidence is weak. Second, the threat of Tylenol litigation shadows, and not atypical integration hiccups might sabotage some of the efficiency gains. I prefer to see them just as a bonus, not as a given.

As seen above, the pro forma sales will be around $32 billion. At current figures, KMB will bring in $1.6–1.8 billion in FCF, while KVUE should contribute the same, though on the lower end with $1.6 billion. To be conservative, I assume the combined FCF will be $3.2 billion.

KMB currently has $6.7 billion in net debt, and it will “inherit” KVUE’s $7.5 billion, for a pro forma $14.2 billion. However, as the deal has a cash component of $3.50 per KVUE shares (equivalent to almost $7 billion, with 1.9 billion KVUE shares outstanding), I expect total net financial debt to be $21 billion. KMB confirmed financing is secured.

That’s quite a lot — more than 6x FCF, closer to 7x leverage, assuming both companies do not delever meaningfully until the deal is pulled through. As shown in my KVUE analysis, but also further above here for KMB, both companies pay out practically their entire FCFs in dividends. Together with challenged everyday businesses, substantial deleveraging is rather unrealistic for both on a standalone basis.

This is a project for post combination. Current pro forma EBITDA is around $7 billion, resulting in leverage of about 2.5x, which management is eyeing. Management is targeting to reach 2x within two years.

I have been wondering how this could be achieved if both have issues to grow, margin pressure, while paying out their entire FCF in dividends (KMB almost, KVUE fully). Management plans with synergies. I would not take it for granted that everything runs smoothly. Growth seems to be a risky bet either way, in my opinion.

My expectation is that KVUE’s current dividend, which should have been cut already in my view, will be right-sized post-deal (as I expected this to happen soon on a standalone basis). In other words, KMB buys KVUE and then cuts their dividend in order not to have to lower their own payout. I have shown the numbers above — dividend coverage in both cases is very thin.

It would not be a novelty, as other companies like 3M (MMM) or W.P. Carey (WPC) have used their respective transformational deals (however spin-offs) to cut their shareholder distributions, as debt was pressing.

This could catch one or the other investor on the wrong foot. Better be prepared. Especially, if the underlying businesses continue to underperform and to struggle as they have done recently.

With this, a dividend cut would clearly destroy shareholder value. But what about the valuation? KMB has an EV of $40 billion KVUE of $48 billion (at the takeover price) for a combined EV of $88 billion. My view is that $3.2 billion in expected FCF does not create a compelling enough setup to invest into to challenged on their own challenged entities. The resulting multiple is 27-28x — way too much for me, given the huge debt load, execution risks, a potential and not unlikely dividend cut, as well as ongoing challenges.

Accordingly, KMB stock is a strong sell.

Risks to My Thesis

My strong sell vote is only valid under the assumption the deal passes. If regulators cancel it or a higher bid emerges (and closes), KMB stock could see a strong relief rally. The first reaction to the deal was strongly negative; hence, I expect a 10–15% move up if the deal does not close.

If management over-delivers on their synergy targets and blends in some growth, the combined entities could do better than I am currently expecting.

Consumer sentiment is currently weak — an improvement with higher spending and a comeback of premium brands could increase sales and margins, for a twin-turbo pushing the business forward.

Conclusion

KMB stock is a strong sell if the deal closes: high debt meets a high valuation, execution risks, and potentially a dividend cut if consumer weakness persists.
